period: c. 632 CE – present (collection, criticism, and transmission)
title: The Hadith
summary: The vast body of reported words, deeds, and silent approvals of the Prophet Muhammad — transmitted through chains of named witnesses, sifted by an elaborate science of authentication, and gathered into canonical collections — that stands after the Quran as the second source of Islamic law and practice for mainstream Sunni and Shia Islam, though the accepted collections differ.
hook: How do you pray five times a day when the book that commands it barely says how? The Quran commands prayer and marks its times, but it does not lay out the full ritual form: the postures, the units, the words. For that, and for a thousand other questions of daily life, Muslims turned to the example of the Prophet — remembered, repeated, and written down as short reports called hadith. Each report arrives with a credential attached: a chain of names tracing it back, witness by witness, to the man who said it. Over centuries, scholars built one of the most demanding systems any premodern culture devised for deciding which of those chains to trust.

plainEnglish.theText:
The hadith are short reports about the Prophet Muhammad. Each one tells what he said, did, or quietly allowed. After the Quran, the hadith are the second source of guidance in Islam. Together, the Prophet's example is called his sunna. This word means his trodden path or way.

A simple question shows why the hadith matter. The Quran, the holy book of Islam, tells believers to pray. But it does not say how to stand, when to bow, or what to say. So where do the answers come from? They come from what Muslims remembered about the Prophet. They watched how he prayed and passed it on.

Each hadith has two parts. The first part is a chain of names. It might read: "A heard from B, who heard from C, that the Prophet said..." This chain is called the isnad. The second part is the actual saying or story. That part is called the matn. Muslims learned to study both parts with great care.
plainEnglish.momentOfMaking:
When the Prophet died in 632, he left no book of his own sayings behind. His followers carried his words in their memories. As they spread across new lands, they repeated what they had seen and heard. Soon there were thousands upon thousands of reports in circulation.

This created a problem. Many reports were honest memories. But others were invented. Some people made up sayings to win an argument, to support a ruler, or to push people toward good behavior. A false hadith could change a law or a belief. So how could anyone tell the true reports from the false ones?

Muslim scholars answered with a huge effort of investigation. They asked: who passed this report along? Were those people honest? Did they have good memories? Could each one have really met the next person in the chain? Scholars traveled for years collecting reports and checking the people in them. In the 800s, two scholars named al-Bukhari and Muslim each built a famous collection of reports they judged sound. For Sunni Muslims, these two books became the most trusted of all.
plainEnglish.whatItClaims:
The hadith claim to preserve the example of the best human being, the Prophet Muhammad. For Muslims, this example is not just history. It is a guide for how to live now.

The Quran gives broad commands. Pray. Give to the poor. Fast in the month of Ramadan. The hadith fill in the details. They show how the Prophet washed before prayer, how he treated his neighbors, how he ate and slept and forgave. From these reports, scholars worked out Islamic law. These are the rules that shape worship and daily life.

Not every report carries the same weight. Scholars sorted them into grades. A report with a strong, trusted chain was called sound, or sahih. A slightly weaker one was good, or hasan. A doubtful one was weak. A report shown to be invented was called fabricated. A sound hadith could carry weight in settling a question. A fabricated one had to be thrown out. This sorting was the heart of the whole effort.
plainEnglish.interpretiveEcosystem:
The science of checking hadith grew into a whole field of study. Scholars wrote long books listing the people who passed reports along. They recorded who was honest, who was careless, and who could not be trusted. This work was sometimes called the science of men, because it judged the human links in each chain.

Not all Muslims built their collections the same way. The largest group, called Sunni Muslims, treasure the collections of al-Bukhari and Muslim above all, along with four other books often grouped as the Six Books. Shia Muslims, a different branch, made their own collections. They give special trust to reports passed down through the Prophet's family and its later leaders. The split between these branches is its own long story.

In modern times, some scholars in the West began to ask hard questions. They wondered how many reports really go back to the Prophet, and how many were added later. A few modern Muslim movements went further and said only the Quran should count. But most Muslims hold the sound collections as a true and binding guide.
plainEnglish.usedAndWeaponized:
The hadith shape Islamic law, so they carry real power. And power invites misuse. From the earliest years, people understood that a saying placed in the Prophet's mouth could move a crowd or settle a fight. That is exactly why some invented them.

Rulers wanted hadith that praised their rule. Rival groups wanted hadith that backed their side. Even well-meaning preachers sometimes made up sayings to scare people away from sin or draw them toward prayer. The Prophet himself was remembered to have warned that lying about him was a grave act. Scholars repeated that warning often.

The science of checking reports was the answer to this danger. It was a shield against forgery. Still, debate never fully stopped. Different schools of law leaned on different reports. A saying one group accepted, another might call weak. Today, when people quote a hadith to support a cause, careful Muslims still ask the old question first. Who passed this down, and can the chain be trusted? The credential attached to the report has always been as important as the words.

beginner.theText:
A Muslim wants to know how to pray. The Quran, the holy book of Islam, tells believers to pray. But it does not say how to stand, when to bow, or what to say. So where does the answer come from? It comes from the example of the Prophet Muhammad. Muslims watched how he prayed and remembered it. They passed it on. Those memories of his words and actions are called the hadith.

A hadith is a short report. It tells what the Prophet said, did, or quietly allowed. After the Quran, the hadith are the second source of guidance in Islam. Together, the Prophet's example is called his sunna, which means his trodden path or way.

Each hadith comes in two parts. The first part is a chain of names. It might read: "A heard from B, who heard from C, that the Prophet said..." This chain is called the isnad. The second part is the actual saying or story. That part is called the matn. Muslims learned to study both parts with great care.

This created a problem. Many reports were honest memories. But others were invented. Some people made up sayings to win an argument, to support a ruler, or to push people toward good behavior. A false hadith could change a law or a belief. So how could anyone tell the true reports from the false ones?

Muslim scholars answered with a huge effort of investigation. They asked: who passed this report along? Were those people honest? Did they have good memories? Could each one have really met the next person in the chain? Scholars traveled for years collecting reports and checking the people in them. In the 800s, two scholars named al-Bukhari and Muslim each built a famous collection of reports they judged sound. For Sunni Muslims, these two books became the most trusted of all.

Rulers wanted hadith that praised their rule. Rival groups wanted hadith that backed their side. general.theText:
The hadith are the remembered reports of what the Prophet Muhammad said, did, and silently approved — the record his community turned to whenever the Quran commanded something, like prayer, without spelling out how. That gap, between a book of broad commands and a life of detailed obligation, is what the hadith were assembled to fill.

A hadith is a single report of what the Prophet said, did, or quietly approved, and together these reports form the sunna, the second source of Islamic law and practice after the Quran. Each report has two parts that scholars learned to weigh separately. The isnad is the chain of transmitters, the named people who passed the report along, one to the next, back to an eyewitness. The matn is the text, the content of what was said or done. Both the power and the difficulty of the tradition lie in the isnad, a way of citing sources developed long before the modern footnote.
general.momentOfMaking:
The Prophet's death in 632 left his followers with abundant guidance but no second scripture to set beside the Quran. What he left was a life, held only in the memories of those who had known him, and within a few generations that memory had swelled into a huge and unstable mass of reports. Some were faithful. Others were stretched, embellished, or simply invented to serve a doctrine, a ruling family, or a pious purpose.

The scholarly answer was the science of hadith criticism, one of the boldest intellectual projects of its age. Its central method was to scrutinize the isnad. Specialists compiled vast biographical dictionaries rating each transmitter for honesty, care, and memory, and asking whether two named links could plausibly have met at all. A report whose chain held up was graded sound, or sahih; a weaker one good, or hasan; a doubtful one weak, or da'if; a proven forgery fabricated, or mawdu'.

In the ninth century this effort produced its monuments. Al-Bukhari and Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj each assembled a collection, both titled Sahih, of reports they judged sound. These two works, joined by four more, came to be grouped by most Sunni Muslims as the Six Books. The sifting was severe: al-Bukhari is said to have weighed an enormous number of reports and kept relatively few.
general.whatItClaims:
The hadith claim to carry the sunna, the example of the man Muslims regard as the finest model of human conduct. This is a claim about authority rather than merely about the past. Where the Quran legislates in broad strokes, the sunna supplies the working detail, and the two together form the base from which scholars derived Islamic law.

The relationship between the two sources is intricate. The hadith are understood to explain the Quran, to specify what it leaves general, and on some questions to reach beyond it. Classical legal theory granted the authenticated sunna binding force, which is precisely why the soundness of any single report mattered so much. A sound hadith could establish an obligation, while a weak one could not bear that weight.

This is also where the Sunni and Shia traditions part ways. Shia Islam grants special authority to reports passed through the Prophet's family, the Ahl al-Bayt, and through the Imams it regards as their rightful heirs. It relies on its own collections rather than the Sunni Six Books. The deeper schism behind this difference is a separate story; here it is enough to note that the question of whose chain to trust sits at the heart of both traditions.
general.interpretiveEcosystem:
Few learned enterprises in the medieval world matched the scale of hadith scholarship. To judge thousands of transmitters, scholars built the science of men. This discipline recorded the reliability of nearly everyone who had ever passed a report along, and its biographical dictionaries ran to many volumes. The grade of a single report could turn on whether one obscure link had a reputation for a faulty memory.

This traditional science still holds authority for most Muslims, who treat the sound collections as a trustworthy guide to the Prophet's example. Beside it, a very different conversation developed in Western universities from the late nineteenth century onward. The scholar Ignaz Goldziher argued that many hadith reflected the controversies of later generations, projected back onto the Prophet. Joseph Schacht pressed the same skepticism onto legal reports in particular. Later scholars such as Harald Motzki pushed back, developing methods to trace some material to earlier dates than the skeptics had allowed.

These are not the same project, and they ask different questions. The traditional science asks which reports are sound enough to ground religious practice. The academic debate asks how much of the corpus can be shown, by external methods, to go back to the Prophet himself. A small modern movement, sometimes called Quranist, rejects hadith authority altogether and looks to the Quran alone. It remains a minority far outside the mainstream of Muslim practice.
general.usedAndWeaponized:
Authority attracts forgery, and the hadith were authoritative almost from the start. A report attributed to the Prophet could settle a point of law, prop up a ruler, or shame a rival. The incentive to fabricate was constant, and the scholars who fought it understood it well. The tradition itself preserved a warning, widely transmitted, that to lie deliberately in the Prophet's name was a grave offense.

Forgery served many masters. Ruling families wanted reports that legitimized their rule, and rival sects and legal schools wanted reports that favored their positions. Some preachers invented sayings to move audiences toward virtue, a motive they may have thought harmless. The biographical and isnad sciences were, in large part, the disciplined answer to this pressure, designed to expose invented chains and isolate the reliable core.

Yet the science did not end disagreement so much as give it shape. The various schools of law accepted somewhat different bodies of reports and weighted them differently. A hadith treated as decisive in one tradition might be set aside as weak in another. This continues into the present. When a hadith is quoted in a modern argument over law, gender, or politics, careful Muslims still ask after its grade and its chain. The credential travels with the text, and judging it remains, as it always was, the discipline's central act.

advanced.theText:
The scholarly study of hadith is organized around a feature with no exact parallel in other scriptural traditions: the isnad. This transmission chain, appended to each report, purports to document its descent from the Prophet through a sequence of named guarantors. The classical sciences treated the isnad as the primary object of evaluation. They developed a taxonomy of chains (continuous, broken, elevated, single-strand) and a graded vocabulary of authenticity in which the rating of the matn followed largely from the rating of its transmitters. Western scholarship, by contrast, has tended to treat the isnad itself as the central historical problem, asking whether the chains are reliable records of transmission or later constructions that lend reports a fictive pedigree.

This divergence frames the modern field. The canonical Sunni corpus crystallized around the two Sahihs and the Six Books in the ninth and tenth centuries. But the body of material those works selected from was vastly larger, and the criteria by which compilers like al-Bukhari included or excluded reports are themselves a subject of close study. Jonathan A.C. Brown's work on canonization traces how the two Sahihs acquired their near-scriptural standing over the centuries that followed their composition, a status not self-evident at the moment they were written.
advanced.momentOfMaking:
The compositional question that has driven a century of Western scholarship is the dating of the corpus. How much of the material attributed to the Prophet can be shown to originate in the early seventh century, rather than in the controversies of the eighth and ninth? The test must rely on methods external to the tradition's own claims. Ignaz Goldziher argued that hadith largely reflect the views of later generations. Joseph Schacht advanced a more specific thesis: that legal traditions grew backward, projecting the doctrines of later schools onto progressively earlier authorities and finally onto the Prophet. Together they set the terms of a deeply skeptical position.

The most consequential methodological response has been the isnad-cum-matn analysis associated with Harald Motzki and others, which correlates variations in the wording of parallel reports with variations in their chains. Where multiple independent chains converge on a single early figure, the so-called common link, the method infers that the report was in circulation by that figure's generation. This pushes some material earlier than Schacht's chronology allowed. The interpretation of the common link remains contested: Schacht read it as the point of fabrication, while Motzki and his followers read it as a genuine point of dissemination. The debate is not resolved, and what is at stake is precisely the evidentiary status of the isnad as a historical record.
advanced.whatItClaims:
The juristic claim embedded in the hadith corpus is that the authenticated sunna possesses independent legislative authority alongside the Quran, a claim whose consolidation was itself a historical achievement rather than an original given. Wael Hallaq and others have traced how legal reasoning came to be systematically subordinated to prophetic precedent, a development conventionally associated with al-Shafi'i. This established the sunna as a formal source of law and thereby raised the stakes of authentication to their classical pitch. Once the Prophet's example was binding, the grade of a report ceased to be an academic nicety and became a question of legal force.

This generates a tension the tradition has long negotiated: the relation between the technical authenticity of a report's chain and the substantive acceptability of its content. Jurists were not bound to act on every sound hadith. They sometimes set aside well-attested reports on grounds of inconsistency with stronger evidence or established principle, just as they sometimes admitted weak reports for non-legal purposes such as moral exhortation. The Shia traditions complicate the picture further. They ground authority in transmission through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt and apply their own criteria of evaluation. The very definition of a reliable chain thus differs across the sectarian divide whose origins lie elsewhere.
advanced.interpretiveEcosystem:
The mature science of transmitter criticism assessed each link in every chain for probity and accuracy. It constitutes one of the most elaborate evaluative apparatuses produced by any pre-modern scholarly culture, and its internal logic repays analysis on its own terms rather than as a foil for modern method. Its biographical literature did not merely record reputations. It encoded a theory of testimony, weighing the conditions under which a human report may be trusted, and its practitioners disputed those conditions vigorously among themselves. The frequent assumption that traditional criticism was credulous, content to certify chains while ignoring content, understates the sophistication with which leading critics also scrutinized the plausibility of the matn.

The canon question sharpens when the Sunni and Shia trajectories are set side by side. The two communities did not merely grade shared reports differently. They assembled substantially different corpora, the Sunni Six Books and the Shia collections such as al-Kulayni's al-Kafi, resting on partly distinct registers of acceptable transmitters. This means that the authority of a hadith is not a property of the text alone. It is a property of a text-within-a-tradition, certified by a community's particular account of who could be trusted to carry it. The canon, on this view, is the sedimented outcome of communal judgment rather than a neutral discovery.
advanced.usedAndWeaponized:
The genuinely uncomfortable problem at the center of the field is that the traditional science and the academic inquiry cannot be reconciled simply by declaring them to ask different questions. At some points they make incompatible claims about the same object. Consider a report bearing a chain that the classical critics certified as sound and that a skeptical reading regards as a back-projection. Both verdicts concern whether particular words go back to the Prophet, and they cannot both be wholly right. The convenient formula that faith and history occupy separate domains breaks down precisely where the historical claim of an isnad meets the historical method of the critic.

The stakes are not only scholarly. Because the corpus underwrites law, a determination about authenticity can bear on contested questions of gender, governance, violence, and ritual. Modern reformers and traditionalists alike have reached for the tools of authentication to license or to resist particular readings. Some reformers revive the classical critics' own willingness to question content, arguing that a report inconsistent with the Quran's ethical thrust may be set aside whatever its chain. Traditionalists reply that such selective skepticism, untethered from the formal science, collapses into reading one's prior commitments into the sources. The Quranist rejection of the corpus tout court represents the limit case of this argument, and its marginality measures how deeply the sunna remains woven into the structure of Islamic authority. What neither the traditional science nor its modern critics can finally supply is a position outside the contested evidence from which to adjudicate the whole. That is why the dating of the corpus may remain, in its largest form, genuinely open.

foundation.theStory: The hadith pose a historical problem of a distinctive kind: how a community preserves, transmits, and authenticates the memory of a founder across generations, in the absence of a second written scripture from his own hand. After the Prophet Muhammad died in 632, his example, his sunna, survived as a sea of oral reports, many faithful and many fabricated. The story of the hadith is the story of how Muslims built a critical apparatus to separate the two, and how the collections that resulted came to function as the second foundation of Islamic law and life.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debate concerns dating and authenticity: how much of the corpus can be shown to originate with the Prophet rather than with the controversies of later generations. The traditional science of hadith criticism authenticated reports by scrutinizing their chains of transmitters and graded them as sound, good, weak, or fabricated. Western scholarship, from Goldziher and Schacht to Motzki and his successors, has debated whether those chains are reliable historical records or later constructions. The two inquiries ask overlapping but not identical questions, and where they conflict is where the deepest difficulty lies.
foundation.whyItMatters: Understanding the hadith unlocks how Islamic law and practice actually work, why the Quran alone does not determine them, and how a textual tradition can sustain an enormous interpretive enterprise. It illuminates the difference between the Sunni and Shia communities, which canonized different collections; it offers a striking case of pre-modern source criticism; and it frames a live question, faced by many scriptural traditions, about the relationship between a community's account of its sources and a historian's.
questions: The science of hadith criticism judged a report mainly by examining its chain of transmitters rather than its content. What are the strengths of authenticating a report by its chain of witnesses, and what kinds of error might that method miss?

Sunni and Shia Muslims accept substantially different collections of hadith, in part because they trust different transmitters. What does it mean for the authority of a text to depend not on the text alone but on a community's judgment about who can be trusted to carry it?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The conversation runs between two traditions that rarely share premises. The classical science of hadith, codified in the biographical literature and the manuals of transmitter criticism, evaluated chains and assigned grades within a framework that accepted the sunna's authority. Modern academic study, founded by Ignaz Goldziher and sharpened by Joseph Schacht for legal reports, questioned whether the chains record transmission at all. Harald Motzki's isnad-cum-matn analysis and Jonathan A.C. Brown's work on how the two Sahihs were canonized represent the more recent effort to recover what can be said historically without simply endorsing either the tradition's claims or the early skeptics' dismissals.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence consists of the reports themselves, surviving in multiple collections with their chains; the vast biographical literature assessing transmitters; and comparison of parallel versions of the same report across different chains. The limits are severe. There are few external sources against which to check an isnad, the earliest written collections postdate the Prophet by generations, and the period of primarily oral transmission is the very stretch the chains are meant to document and that critics most doubt. Method can sometimes establish that a report was circulating by a given generation, but rarely that its words are the Prophet's own.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument must resist the easy settlement that faith and history simply ask different questions, because the authenticity of a specific report is a claim both make. It might proceed, as the common-link analyses do, by showing that a particular report can be traced to an early disseminator, and then arguing carefully about what that descent does and does not prove. The honest position specifies what the available evidence can establish, such as the approximate date a report entered circulation; what it cannot, such as verbatim attribution to the Prophet; and why the largest version of the question, the authenticity of the corpus as a whole, may remain open.
questions: Schacht argued that legal hadith tended to grow backward, with later doctrines attributed to progressively earlier authorities and finally to the Prophet. What evidence would support this back-projection thesis, and what evidence, such as Motzki's common-link findings, would tell against it?

The traditional science is often caricatured as certifying chains while ignoring content. Given that leading classical critics also weighed the plausibility of a report's content, how should a historian assess the sophistication of pre-modern hadith criticism on its own terms?

research.topic: The structure of the hadith and the formation of the Sunni canon
research.content: It is well established that a hadith consists of an isnad (chain of transmitters) and a matn (text), and that the classical Sunni canon coalesced in the ninth and tenth centuries around the two Sahih collections of al-Bukhari (d. 870) and Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj (d. 875), later grouped with four further works as the Six Books (al-Kutub al-Sitta). The science of hadith criticism developed a graded vocabulary, sahih (sound), hasan (good), da'if (weak), and mawdu' (fabricated), in which authentication turned primarily on evaluation of the transmitters through a large biographical literature (ilm al-rijal). Jonathan A.C. Brown's The Canonization of al-Bukhari and Muslim (2007) documents how the two Sahihs acquired their authoritative standing over subsequent centuries rather than instantly upon composition. The Shia traditions canonized distinct collections, including al-Kulayni's al-Kafi, and apply their own criteria of transmitter reliability.

research.topic: Isnad-cum-matn analysis and the dating of individual reports
research.content: A significant body of recent scholarship has developed methods for dating individual reports by correlating variations in their wording (matn) with variations in their chains (isnad). The isnad-cum-matn approach, associated above all with Harald Motzki and developed by scholars such as Gregor Schoeler and Andreas Gorke, identifies a common link, a transmitter on whom multiple independent chains converge, and infers that a report was in circulation by that figure's generation. Applied to specific traditions, the method has dated some material earlier than the radical skepticism of Joseph Schacht allowed. The interpretation of the common link remains genuinely contested, however: Schacht read it as the likely point of fabrication, while Motzki read it as a point of genuine dissemination. The field is actively developing, and conclusions remain specific to particular reports rather than the corpus as a whole.

research.topic: Whether the words of a given hadith verbatim originate with the Prophet Muhammad
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: contemporaneous written records from the Prophet's lifetime (d. 632) documenting his statements, or independent external attestation of specific sayings from the first decades after his death, against which the later chains could be checked. Why that evidence is unavailable: the earliest comprehensive written collections postdate the Prophet by roughly two centuries; the formative period of transmission was substantially oral; and there are few sources external to the tradition against which an isnad can be verified, since the chains are themselves the principal record of the transmission they claim to document. What can be responsibly inferred: isnad-cum-matn analysis can sometimes establish that a particular report was in circulation by a given early generation, narrowing the window of possible origin; and the traditional sciences identify a core of reports whose chains the classical critics judged exceptionally strong. What must remain genuinely unknowable: whether the specific wording of most individual reports reproduces the Prophet's own speech, as opposed to a faithful paraphrase, a later reformulation, or an attribution, cannot in most cases be established by available methods. This is a question about which the traditional science and academic inquiry reach their respective limits, and it should be presented as such rather than adjudicated as a matter of faith.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Authenticated Sunna
comparativeNarrative.content: For Sunni Muslims, the hadith carry the sunna, the Prophet's example, as the second guide after the Quran. Classical scholars built a strict science to test them. They studied the chain of transmitters behind each report and graded it sound, good, weak, or fabricated. The two Sahih collections of al-Bukhari and Muslim, with the rest of the Six Books, stand as the trusted core. On this view, the work of checking transmitters was a real achievement. It filtered an unstable mass of reports down to a reliable body, and a sound chain is what lets a report ground law and practice. The collections are not seen as a lucky guess. They are the careful product of many generations, and they hold authority for most Muslims today.

comparativeNarrative.name: Transmission Through the Family
comparativeNarrative.content: Shia Muslims agree that the Prophet's example is binding. But they place its reliable handing-down somewhere else. Reports carried through the Prophet's family, the Ahl al-Bayt, and through the Imams the Shia see as their rightful heirs, hold special authority. Shia scholars built their own collections, among them al-Kulayni's al-Kafi, resting on a partly different list of trusted transmitters. The difference is not just which reports to accept. It is whose word counts as trustworthy in the first place. This view shows something the Sunni account can hide: the canon of hadith cannot be split from a community's judgment about who could carry the Prophet's words. The roots of the divide lie in the early dispute over leadership, told elsewhere. The same founder yields two distinct bodies of guidance.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Chain as Historical Problem
comparativeNarrative.content: Western academic scholars treat the hadith as historians treat any reported testimony. They ask when each report can be shown to have begun, and how far the chains can be trusted as records of who told whom. Ignaz Goldziher opened this line, and Joseph Schacht sharpened it, arguing that much of the material reflects the quarrels of later generations, read back onto the Prophet. Later scholars such as Harald Motzki built finer methods that traced some reports to earlier dates than the skeptics had allowed. This view does not rule on whether the hadith are religiously binding, a question outside its reach. It asks a narrower historical one. Its hardest finding is that the chains the tradition treats as credentials are the very thing a historian most struggles to confirm.

comparativeSummary: The same reports sit before three sets of readers who do not agree on what a chain of names can prove. To the Sunni tradition, a sound chain is a hard-won credential that lets a report shape law. To the Shia tradition, the chain matters mainly as it runs through the Prophet's family, so the same founder yields a different canon. To Western scholarship, the chain is not a credential at all but the central puzzle, the thing most in need of checking and least able to be checked from outside. The disagreement is not finally about which reports are true. It is about what the isnad is: a guarantee, a line of trust, or a claim that still awaits proof. No vantage stands above the other two to settle it.

rabbitHole.label: Muhammad
rabbitHole.reason: The hadith record the words, deeds, and tacit approvals of the Prophet Muhammad; his example, the sunna, is the entire object the collections preserve and the criticism seeks to authenticate.

rabbitHole.label: The Quran
rabbitHole.reason: The Quran is the first scripture of Islam; the hadith stand as the second source of law and practice alongside it, supplying the operative detail the Quran's broad commands leave open.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Islamic law and daily practice rest on the hadith as much as on the Quran; understanding the faith requires understanding how the Prophet's remembered example became binding.

rabbitHole.label: The Sunni-Shia Split
rabbitHole.reason: Sunni and Shia communities canonized substantially different hadith collections and trust different transmitters, so the schism produced two distinct bodies of authoritative prophetic tradition.

rabbitHole.label: The Islamic Golden Age
rabbitHole.reason: The science of hadith criticism, with its biographical dictionaries and graded vocabulary of authenticity, was one of the great scholarly enterprises of the era's intellectual flowering.

rabbitHole.label: Sufism
rabbitHole.reason: Sufi piety drew heavily on hadith, including the contested category of sacred hadith in which the Prophet relays words attributed to God, raising distinctive questions of authentication.

rabbitHole.label: Sharia
rabbitHole.reason: Islamic law is derived in large part from the authenticated sunna; the grade assigned to a hadith bears directly on whether it can establish a legal obligation.

reference.author: Brown, Jonathan A.C.
reference.title: Hadith: Muhammad's Legacy in the Medieval and Modern World
reference.annotation: The single best scholarly introduction in English to the hadith, the science of their authentication, and the modern debates around them. Brown presents the traditional Muslim science and the Western academic inquiry side by side without collapsing either into the other, which is exactly the balance this entry attempts.

reference.author: Brown, Jonathan A.C.
reference.title: The Canonization of al-Bukhari and Muslim: The Formation and Function of the Sunni Hadith Canon
reference.annotation: The detailed study underpinning this entry's claim that the two Sahihs acquired their near-scriptural authority over centuries rather than at the moment of composition; named in the research note and the advanced layer as the field's account of how the Sunni canon was made.

reference.author: Motzki, Harald
reference.title: The Origins of Islamic Jurisprudence: Meccan Fiqh before the Classical Schools
reference.annotation: The work developing the isnad-cum-matn and common-link methods that the entry credits with dating some reports earlier than Schacht allowed; foundational for the academic side of the authenticity debate the advanced and research layers describe.

reference.author: Schacht, Joseph
reference.title: The Origins of Muhammadan Jurisprudence
reference.annotation: The classic statement of the skeptical thesis that legal hadith grew backward, with later doctrines attributed to progressively earlier authorities and finally to the Prophet; the entry presents it as the position later common-link analysis was developed to test.

reference.author: Goldziher, Ignaz
reference.title: Muslim Studies (Muhammedanische Studien), Volume 2
reference.annotation: The founding work of Western critical study of hadith, arguing that much of the corpus reflects the controversies of later generations rather than the Prophet's lifetime; the entry treats it as the origin of the academic debate it later complicates.

reference.author: Hallaq, Wael B.
reference.title: The Origins and Evolution of Islamic Law
reference.annotation: The source for the entry's account of how the authenticated sunna was consolidated as a formal, binding source of law, the development that raised the stakes of hadith authentication to their classical pitch; supports the advanced layer's argument about why the grade of a report carried legal force.

reference.author: Siddiqi, Muhammad Zubayr
reference.title: Hadith Literature: Its Origin, Development and Special Features
reference.annotation: A clear scholarly survey of the classical hadith sciences from within the discipline's own framework, including the categories of grading and the biographical literature; useful for representing the traditional science on its own terms rather than only as a foil for modern method.

commerce.title: Hadith: Muhammad's Legacy in the Medieval and Modern World
commerce.author: Jonathan A.C. Brown
commerce.note: The book to open first if you have never studied the hadith. Brown explains what a hadith is, how the science of authentication works, and why modern scholars argue about it, in prose a curious non-specialist can follow without prior background.

commerce.title: An Introduction to the Science of the Hadith
commerce.author: Ibn al-Salah al-Shahrazuri
commerce.note: For the reader who wants the classical science in the voice of the tradition itself. This medieval manual is the foundational handbook of hadith criticism; read it to see how Muslim scholars defined soundness, weakness, and the grades in between.

commerce.title: Hadith Literature: Its Origin, Development and Special Features
commerce.author: Muhammad Zubayr Siddiqi
commerce.note: Good for the reader ready to go past a first overview. A compact scholarly account of how the collections were assembled and how the transmitter sciences operated, written from inside the discipline's framework.

commerce.title: The Canonization of al-Bukhari and Muslim: The Formation and Function of the Sunni Hadith Canon
commerce.author: Jonathan A.C. Brown
commerce.note: For serious students of how a canon is made. A demanding academic study of how the two Sahih collections rose to their authoritative status over centuries; assumes you already know the basics this entry lays out.

commerce.title: The Origins of Islamic Jurisprudence: Meccan Fiqh before the Classical Schools
commerce.author: Harald Motzki
commerce.note: For the reader who wants to follow the dating debate to its technical edge. Motzki develops the methods used to argue that some reports circulated earlier than the skeptics claimed; rigorous and not a first book on the subject.

popularCulture.title: "Actions are judged by intentions"
popularCulture.description: One of the most quoted hadith of all opens al-Bukhari's great collection: a deed is judged by the intention behind it. Muslims cite it in sermons, schoolbooks, and daily talk to mean that what you mean to do counts as much as what you do. The short line travels far from its setting. Few who quote it picture the long chain of named witnesses that scholars studied to call it sound.

popularCulture.title: Hadith apps and the search box
popularCulture.description: Phone apps and websites now let anyone type a topic and pull up hadith in seconds, often with a grade like sound or weak attached. This puts a huge tradition in everyone's pocket. It also flattens it. A grade that scholars reached through years of study becomes a small label next to a verse, and the hard question of who decided that grade, and how, slips out of view.

popularCulture.title: Quoting the Prophet in public debate
popularCulture.description: When Muslims argue about how to live, dress, vote, or treat others, someone usually quotes a hadith. The Prophet's remembered words carry weight no ordinary opinion can. But a saying that one group calls sound, another may call weak or even invented. The same report can be pulled in opposite directions, which is why careful people still ask first who passed it down.

popularCulture.title: The warning against lying about the Prophet
popularCulture.description: A famous report has the Prophet warn that whoever lies about him on purpose has done something grave. The line shows up often in talks about honesty and rumor. It also marks a turning point in the tradition's own story. The danger of fake sayings was so real that the warning against them became one of the most repeated sayings of all.

popularCulture.title: "Sacred hadith" in Sufi devotion
popularCulture.description: A special kind of report, called sacred hadith, has the Prophet relay words spoken by God yet not part of the Quran. Sufis, Muslims drawn to the inner life of faith, treasured these for their warmth and closeness. One says God is nearer to a person than their own neck vein. These lines move many hearts, though scholars long debated how to weigh and authenticate them.
